description: Quest chains shown on the /onboarding page (native + web both load this file). Data-driven so new quests can be added without touching page code. Progress is tracked per step in the user's localStorage under 'humanity_quest_progress'. v0.367: reworded to be UNIVERSAL, each quest must read as something that applies everywhere on Earth, in any climate or economy, with no place-specific or unverifiable claims and no single-nation stats or currency.

chains.title: First Steps
chains.description: Get set up in under five minutes. Your identity is created automatically when you open the chat.

steps.title: Say hello
steps.description: Open the chat, pick a display name, and introduce yourself in #welcome. Your cryptographic identity is created on your device automatically. No signup, no email, no password.

steps.title: Set up your profile
steps.description: Add a bio and let people know what skills you have or what you want to learn. Are you good with tools? Know about gardening? Interested in solar?

steps.title: Back up your seed phrase
steps.description: In Settings, write down your 24-word recovery phrase. This is the only way to recover your identity if you lose your device. Store it somewhere safe, offline.

steps.title: Create your first task
steps.description: Open the Tasks page and add something you want to accomplish this week. It can be as simple as 'research rainwater' or 'learn what grows in my climate.'

chains.title: Water Independence
chains.description: Wherever you live, the safest water is water you can collect, filter, and store yourself. Wells run dry, pipes freeze or break, droughts and storms hit, and supplies get cut off or priced out of reach. Learn to never depend on a single source you do not control.

steps.title: Know how much water you use
steps.description: Most people use far more water than they realize, and the amount varies hugely by place and habit. For a few days, track what you actually use for drinking, cooking, cleaning, and growing. Your own baseline is step one.

steps.title: Learn to harvest water where you are
steps.description: Any roof is a catchment, and even modest rainfall adds up over a year. Where rain is scarce, the same instinct applies to dew, fog nets, morning condensation, and protecting every drop that does fall. Check the Resources page for collection guides.

steps.title: Make any water safe to drink
steps.description: From boiling and simple sand-and-charcoal filters you can build by hand, to ceramic gravity filters, to reverse osmosis. Match the method to your water source and what you can afford. The goal is the same everywhere: water you can trust.

steps.title: Store a reserve you can rely on
steps.description: Store at least two weeks of drinking water for everyone in your home, then build toward more. Any clean, food-safe container works, from jugs to large tanks. In freezing climates, store it where it will not burst; in hot ones, store it out of the sun and sealed against pests.

chains.title: Food Independence
chains.description: However much space you have, from a single windowsill to open land, you can grow part of what you eat. Soil beds, containers, and soil-free towers each suit different climates and spaces. Practice in the simulation first, where a failed crop costs nothing, then grow the real thing.

steps.title: Start with what you already eat
steps.description: Greens and herbs grow fast in small spaces and almost any climate. Staples like potatoes, beans, squash, and grains store well and feed you longer. Grow a little of what you love and eat most before you scale up.

steps.title: Try the farming simulation
steps.description: The in-app simulation lets you experiment with what to plant, where, and how to water and feed it, all without spending a thing. Make your mistakes here first, then take what works outside.

steps.title: Plan your first growing setup
steps.description: Your first setup can be nearly free: repurposed containers and local soil, or a simple soil-free tower built from common parts. Start with what is cheap and available where you live. The Resources page has build guides for many climates.

steps.title: Learn about closed-loop systems
steps.description: Aquaponics pairs fish with plants: fish waste feeds the plants, the plants clean the water. One loop yields both protein and vegetables with very little brought in from outside. The same closed-loop thinking scales from a barrel to a greenhouse.

steps.title: Share your surplus
steps.description: Once you grow more than you eat, post the surplus on the Market page and trade with neighbors. A web of growers who share is far harder to starve than any one of them alone.

chains.title: Energy Independence
chains.description: When power fails, much of the rest follows: water pumps, keeping food cold, communication, heating, cooling. Whether you are off the grid by choice, by circumstance, or because there is no grid where you live, learn to generate and store your own power.

steps.title: Cut waste before you generate
steps.description: Find what draws the most power in your home, usually heating, cooling, and refrigeration, and reduce it first. Every unit of energy you do not use is one you never have to produce, store, or pay for.

steps.title: Match generation to your climate
steps.description: Sunlight reaches everywhere, though some places get far more than others. Even a single small solar panel can run lights, charge phones, and keep critical devices alive. Where sun is scarce, wind, flowing water, or human-powered options can carry the load.

steps.title: Understand storage
steps.description: Power is only useful when you need it, so storage matters as much as generation. Modern batteries last for years and thousands of cycles. Size your storage to carry you through the night and the quiet days with no sun or wind.

steps.title: Power your essentials first
steps.description: Decide what absolutely must stay on: keeping food and medicine cold, pumping water, light, communication. Power those first, then expand as you can. Riding out the outage matters more than running everything at once.

chains.title: Build Your Network
chains.description: Self-sufficiency does not mean doing everything alone. The strongest communities are networks of people who each bring different skills and share freely. No one is good at everything, and no one has to be.

steps.title: Find people near you
steps.description: Use the Maps page to find community gardens, workshops, tool libraries, and other people on HumanityOS in your area. Introduce yourself.

steps.title: Share what you know
steps.description: Everyone has something to teach: plumbing, electrical, cooking, childcare, repair, first aid, language, growing. Add your skills to your profile so people can find you.

steps.title: Start a group project
steps.description: A shared garden, a tool co-op, a neighborhood power install, a bulk food buy. Use Tasks to coordinate, Chat to communicate, and Maps to plan it together.

steps.title: Vouch for someone you trust
steps.description: When you vouch for someone, you tell the network that this person is real and trustworthy. It helps everyone tell genuine people from fake accounts, without anyone in charge deciding for them.

chains.title: Going Deeper
chains.description: When you are ready to do more, or to help build the platform itself.

steps.title: Install the desktop app
steps.description: A native app that works offline. Same identity as the web version. It includes the 3D simulation engine for growing, building, and planning.

steps.title: 3D print real tools
steps.description: Models from the simulation can be exported for 3D printing. Design a part in the sim, print it, install it. What you learn in the game builds real tools for the real world.

steps.title: Run your own server
steps.description: One program, under ten minutes from nothing to live. Your community gets its own space, and people keep their identity when they move between servers.

steps.title: Help build HumanityOS
steps.description: Code, writing, design, translation, testing, feedback. This is a public-domain project. Everything built here belongs to everyone.

steps.title: Read the Humanity Accord
steps.description: The foundation everything else rests on. It defines the constraints every system must respect: dignity, consent, transparency, and non-domination. Short and plain-language.
